title: [Ruby] Cannot read message-typed custom options from a rebuilt DescriptorPool (FieldDescriptor#get raises TypeError)
author: cocoahero
body:
## What

Reading a **message-typed** custom option via `FieldDescriptor#get` raises `TypeError` when the extension is resolved from a `DescriptorPool` that was built from serialized `FileDescriptorProto`s (i.e. not the process's generated pool). Scalar custom options read fine in the same setup, and message-typed options read fine from `DescriptorPool.generated_pool`.

This is exactly the situation a `protoc` plugin is in: it receives already-compiled descriptors in a `CodeGeneratorRequest` and must rebuild a pool from them to resolve custom-option extensions.

```
message_opt => raises TypeError: wrong argument type nil (expected Google::Protobuf::Descriptor)
```

## Repro

`repro.proto`:

```proto
syntax = "proto3";
package repro;
import "google/protobuf/descriptor.proto";

message Meta { string owner = 1; }

extend google.protobuf.MessageOptions {
  string scalar_opt = 50001;
  Meta   message_opt = 50002;
}

message Subject {
  option (repro.scalar_opt) = "hello";
  option (repro.message_opt) = { owner: "team" };
}
```

`repro.rb`:

```ruby
require "google/protobuf"
require "google/protobuf/descriptor_pb"

# repro.fds stands in for a CodeGeneratorRequest's proto_file: repro.proto plus
# its dependency, google/protobuf/descriptor.proto.
#   protoc --include_imports --descriptor_set_out=repro.fds repro.proto
fds = Google::Protobuf::FileDescriptorSet.decode(File.read("repro.fds"))

# Rebuild a pool from the received files so the option extensions resolve.
pool = Google::Protobuf::DescriptorPool.new
fds.file.each { |f| pool.add_serialized_file(f.to_proto) }

subject = fds.file.flat_map { |f| f.message_type.to_a }.find { |m| m.name == "Subject" }
options = pool.lookup("google.protobuf.MessageOptions").msgclass.decode(subject.options.to_proto)

scalar_ext  = pool.lookup("repro.scalar_opt")
message_ext = pool.lookup("repro.message_opt")

puts "scalar_opt  present? #{scalar_ext.has?(options)}"   # true
puts "message_opt present? #{message_ext.has?(options)}"  # true

puts "scalar_opt  => #{scalar_ext.get(options).inspect}"  # "hello"
puts "message_opt => #{message_ext.get(options).inspect}" # raises TypeError
```

## Actual

```
scalar_opt  present? true
message_opt present? true
scalar_opt  => "hello"
message_opt => (raises) TypeError: wrong argument type nil (expected Google::Protobuf::Descriptor)
```

The option is present (`has?` is `true`) and round-trips through serialization, but `get` cannot materialize its value.

## Expected

`message_ext.get(options)` returns the `Meta` message `{ owner: "team" }`, the way `scalar_ext.get(options)` returns `"hello"`.

## Key diagnostic

The failure is specific to reading from a **rebuilt** pool. If the same extension is registered directly in `DescriptorPool.generated_pool` (e.g. via generated Ruby code, or by adding the file to the generated pool), `get` returns the message value correctly. It only fails when the extension is resolved from a separately-built `DescriptorPool` — which is the only option available to a `protoc` plugin, since the descriptors in a `CodeGeneratorRequest` are not in the generated pool.

## Relationship to earlier issues

This is a gap left by two previously-closed issues:

- #1198 ("No way to use custom options in Ruby", closed as completed) added the ability to read custom options via `DescriptorPool.generated_pool.lookup(ext_name).get(options)`.
- #16834 ("[Ruby] unable to read custom options from descriptor", closed as completed) documented that same `ext.get(options)` pattern, and a follow-up there gave the guidance for plugins: build your own `DescriptorPool` from the request's files and read through it.

That guidance works for **scalar and repeated-scalar** options, so those issues correctly resolved the common case. It does **not** work for **message-typed** options: `get` raises `TypeError` for them specifically when read from a rebuilt pool, so a plugin still cannot read message-typed custom options (a common shape — e.g. `protovalidate` rules, `google.api.http`). The earlier issues did not cover this case.

## Environment

- google-protobuf **4.35.1** (also reproduces on **4.28.0**)
- libprotoc 33.0
- Ruby 3.4.1
- macOS (arm64-darwin)

editions or proto3 with optional
Java

Currently there are no efficient way to determine whether received Message is empty

Message builder with all fields unset to always build default instance

```java

```

Add any other context or screenshots about the feature request here.

title: Copyright: update year in per-file headers (Debian package libprotobuf-lite32t64)
author: sporteka2
body:
## Copyright year inconsistency in Debian package `libprotobuf-lite32t64`

The Debian copyright file for `libprotobuf-lite32t64` shows:

- `Files: *` Copyright: 2008-2021

But the following file-specific entries have not been updated:

- `Files: python/mox.py` Copyright: 2008-2008 (gap: 13 years)

This was detected by scanning Debian's machine-readable copyright files. The main copyright header was updated to 2021 but individual file entries still show older end years.

Please review and update the copyright headers for the affected files.

title: Inconsistency between bazel and setuptools builds of CPython extension module.
author: mitchallain
body:
The CPython extension module build configuration should provide `extra_compile_args=["-fvisibility=hidden"]` to be equivalent to the Bazel build and to prevent upb symbols leaking into the public interface of the Python extension module. This can create segfaults for applications linking against libprotobuf.so which have duplicate upb symbols and duplicate extension registries

https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/blob/58f02be5e7976f0ed3d84aa9b30f89cb5e47eff6/python/dist/setup.py#L82-L89

The Bazel configuration (note `copts`):

https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/blob/58f02be5e7976f0ed3d84aa9b30f89cb5e47eff6/python/py_extension.bzl#L16-L27

title: protobuf compiler in multiplatform project...
author: Honya2000
body:
Hello,
I have multiplatfrom project which is using hundreds of .proto files.
Recently i realized that adding new .proto files increases wasm binary size by quite sensitive big size.
Investigated binary in the viewer, and found that each proto file have full proto fields text structure inside the binary. Which is unexpected, because i do not need text representation in my code at all.
It turns out protobuf compiler generates Message related proto classes, but there is option to use MessageLite instead.
But problem is it requires to include 
option optimize_for = LITE_RUNTIME
to each .proto file, which isn't option for me bacause:
1. There are too many .proto files and including this option manually will require significant amount of time!
2. As i mentioned the project is multiplatform and includes host tools on windows platform which are used to compile proto graphs. Compiling of protographs require Message based compiled files, because of reflections capabilities.

So i expected some command line switch in proto compiler, so i can compile .proto to Message for host tools and to MessageLite in cross compile configs to reduce the size and to prevent my code from reverse engineering. But surprisingly there are no such command line exists.

Any reason for this?

I guess i will have to modify proto buf compiler code to include this option...

title: Bazel: `'@@rules_rust+//crate_universe:extension.bzl': no such file` since rules_rust 0.71.0
author: rdesgroppes
body:
**What version of protobuf and what language are you using?**
Version: 35.1

Language: Rust

**What supported operating system version are you using (e.g. Linux, Windows)?**
Linux

**What supported runtime / compiler version are you using (e.g. python version, gcc version)**
Bazel 9.1.1 with rules_rust 0.71.0

**What did you do?**
protobuf's MODULE.bazel loads the `crate_universe` extension from `@rules_rust//crate_universe:extension.bzl`.
rules_rust removed that file in 0.71.0 as part of a larger cleanup.
The request for bringing it back for some more time is tracked upstream in bazelbuild/rules_rust#4111.

**What did you expect to see**
A Bazel build that depends on protobuf and uses rules_rust 0.71.0 resolves.

**What did you see instead?**
```
cannot load '@@rules_rust+//crate_universe:extension.bzl': no such file, requested by https://bcr.bazel.build/modules/protobuf/35.1/MODULE.bazel:197:22
```

**Anything else we should know about your project / environment**
The fix is adding one `s`:  `crate_universe/extensions.bzl` has existed since bzlmod support was added, so changing the load to `@rules_rust//crate_universe:extensions.bzl` works on protobuf's current rules_rust (0.69.0) and stays compatible with 0.71.0:
- #28226.

title: Bazel: "`crates` is in a non-root module but has no lockfile" since rules_rust 0.71.1
author: rdesgroppes
body:
**What version of protobuf and what language are you using?**
Version: 35.1

Language: Rust

**What supported operating system version are you using (e.g. Linux, Windows)?**
Linux

**What supported runtime / compiler version are you using (e.g. python version, gcc version)**
Bazel 9.1.1 with rules_rust 0.71.1

**What did you do?**
In a root Bazel module that transitively depends on protobuf, force rules_rust to 0.71.1 and run `bazel mod deps`.
protobuf invokes the `crate_universe` extension unconditionally because it is not a dev dependency, and calls `crate.from_specs()` without a lockfile.

**What did you expect to see**
Resolution succeeds, because protobuf's `crate_universe` usage only supports protobuf's own Rust targets, which consumers do not depend on.

**What did you see instead?**
Resolution fails, because rules_rust 0.71.1 rejects any non-root `crate_universe` call that does not set `lockfile`:
```
crate_universe extension call `crates` is in a non-root module but has no lockfile. Transitive crate_universe repositories must ship a `lockfile = ...` because repinning is not supported across module boundaries.
```

**Anything else we should know about your project / environment**
Would it possible to supply a lockfile?

title: Protobuf `ParseFromArray` takes an `int size` where negative values are UB
author: tejprasadbhattarai638-collab

title: py_proto_library rule not using prebuilt protoc
author: ninepints
body:
**What version of protobuf and what language are you using?**
Version: 35.1
Language: Python

**What [supported operating system version](https://github.com/google/oss-policies-info/tree/main) are you using (e.g. Linux, Windows) ?**
macOS 26.5.1

**What [supported runtime / compiler version](https://github.com/google/oss-policies-info/tree/main) are you using (e.g. python version, gcc version)**
Bazel 9.1.0
rules_python 2.0.0
Python 3.13 toolchain from rules_python

**What did you do?**

I have what I think is a pretty vanilla Bazel/Python/Protobuf configuration, and I'm trying to use the recently-released support for prebuilt protobuf compiler binaries. It's not working - trying to build a `py_proto_library` rule results in the protobuf compiler being built from source.

To debug, I ran `bazel cquery 'somepath("//my_project/proto:my_py_proto", "@protobuf//src/google/protobuf")'` which gave me the following dependency chain:

```
//my_project/proto:my_py_proto
@protobuf//python:python_toolchain
@protobuf//python:protobuf_python
@protobuf//python:python_srcs
@protobuf//python:python_src_files
@protobuf//python:google/protobuf/internal/python_edition_defaults.py
@protobuf//python:embedded_python_edition_defaults_generate
@protobuf//editions:internal_defaults_escape
@protobuf//src/google/protobuf:protobuf
```

My uneducated guess at narrowing down the problem is that `@protobuf//python:python_toolchain` is mis-resolving such that it depends on `@protobuf//python:protobuf_python`, which in turn depends on `python_edition_defaults.py`, in turn generated from C++ stuff. I would expect the dependency chain to stop after `python_toolchain`. To the extent that Python sources are needed to compile my protos, I'd expect them to be part of the prebuilt compiler distribution, and to the extent they're needed at runtime I'd expect to get them from PyPI. But my convictions aren't very strong here, maybe I'm doing something obviously wrong?

For good measure I also tried
```
bazel build //my_project/proto:my_py_proto \
  --'@protobuf//bazel/flags:prefer_prebuilt_protoc' \
  --incompatible_enable_proto_toolchain_resolution
```
with the same result.

**What did you expect to see**

Prebuilt compiler used, compiler not built from source

**What did you see instead?**

Compiler built from source

**Anything else we should know about your project / environment**

I have some gross workarounds in my BUILD files to relocate the proto Python modules going into the wheel I'm trying to build, but all of that is downstream of the `py_proto_library` rule and I wouldn't expect it to affect the proto library's upstream dependencies.

title: 32 bit build with MSC (VS2026) failure compiling upb
author: torsten48
body:
Version: v35.1
Language: C++

Microsoft (R) C/C++ Optimizing Compiler Version 19.51.36243 for x86
(Visual Studio 2026)

building 32bit(!) protobuf via cmake/ninja
```
C:\dev\gi2\extern\protobuf\upb\mem\arena.c(531): error C2118: negative subscript
C:\dev\gi2\extern\protobuf\upb\mem\arena.c(531): error C2148: total size of array must not exceed 0x7fffffff bytes 
```

title: Umbrella issue: protobuf Python Free-Threaded Analysis Report
author: clin1234
body:
Inspired by #26596

**What version of protobuf and what language are you using?**

Version: main

Language: Python

**What
[supported operating system version](https://github.com/google/oss-policies-info/tree/main)
are you using (e.g. Linux, Windows) ?** 

Windows

**What
[supported runtime / compiler version](https://github.com/google/oss-policies-info/tree/main)
are you using (e.g. python version, gcc version)**

Python 3.14

**What did you do?**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

Generated a report using https://github.com/devdanzin/ft-review-toolkit: https://gist.github.com/clin1234/3e88a89013366b77e3b1c06961be3de7

**What did you expect to see**

Perfect free-threading support

**What did you see instead?**

| # | Finding | File:Line | Severity | Backend |
|---|---------|-----------|----------|---------|
| 1 | `allow_oversize_protos` non-atomic `static bool` — written by `SetAllowOversizeProtos`, read by every `MergeFromString` | `pyext/message.cc:408,2018,2039` | CRITICAL | pyext |
| 2 | `WKT_classes` unprotected lazy-init in `message_meta::New` (`tp_new` slot); two threads both see `nullptr`, both write, first ref leaks | `pyext/message.cc:122,260–270` | CRITICAL | pyext |
| 3 | `PyUpb_WeakMap_Next` holds mutex for **entire iteration**; callers invoke Python callbacks (Reify, `Py_DECREF`) inside loop body; **no abort path** — any early exit permanently leaks the mutex | `python/protobuf.c:235–266` | CRITICAL | UPB |
| 4 | `subobj_map->mutex` acquired first by `WeakMap_Next`, then `obj_cache->mutex` acquired inside loop via `ObjCache_Add`; lock ordering `subobj_map → obj_cache` is undocumented and any inversion deadlocks | `python/message.c:769–787`, `protobuf.c:235` | CRITICAL | UPB |
| 5 | `PyUpb_DescriptorBase.options`/`.features` per-object lazy cache with no lock; two threads both see `NULL`, both write, first ref leaks; pyext equivalent is correctly locked with `FreeThreadingLockGuard` | `python/descriptor.c:97–151` | HIGH | UPB |
| 6 | `state->c_descriptor_symtab` unprotected lazy init; two threads race in `GetFileProtoDef`, losing `upb_DefPool*` leaks permanently (C heap, no GC) | `python/descriptor_pool.c:38–43` | HIGH | UPB |
| 7 | `state->wkt_bases` unprotected lazy init with `PyModule_AddObject` side-effect; second racer's `PyModule_AddObject` decrefs first racer's object to 0 → use-after-free | `python/protobuf.c:126–142` | HIGH | UPB |
| 8 | `static int count` inside `upb_trim_allocfunc` — non-atomic `++count`/`count=0` called from every arena free across all threads (Linux/glibc only) | `python/protobuf.c:367–370` | HIGH | UPB |
| 9 | `PyUpb_MessageMeta_GetAttr`/`AddFieldNumber` call `PyObject_SetAttr` on shared type object without `Py_BEGIN_CRITICAL_SECTION`; `AddFieldNumber` writes an integer constant (idempotent — concurrent write of the same value is benign); but `GetAttr` caches freshly-created objects (enum wrappers via `PyObject_CallFunctionObjArgs`) — two threads racing on the same name both mint new wrappers, write to the same key, and the first writer's object is leaked | `python/message.c:2027–2033,2090–2108` | HIGH | UPB |
| 10 | `state->allow_oversize_protos` UPB backend — non-atomic `bool` in `PyUpb_ModuleState`; written by `SetAllowOversizeProtos`, read by message decode path; concurrent R/W in same interpreter | `python/protobuf.h:82`, `protobuf.c:78`, `message.c:1433` | HIGH | UPB |
| 11 | `GetOrCreateMessageClass` TOCTOU: check under lock → release → create outside lock → **never re-registers**; two threads can produce two distinct type objects for the same descriptor, breaking `M is M` identity | `pyext/message_factory.cc:138–198` | HIGH | pyext |
| 12 | `GcClear` calls `Py_CLEAR` on map values while holding non-reentrant `absl::Mutex`; `tp_dealloc` of a message class re-acquires the same mutex → **permanent deadlock** | `pyext/message_factory.cc:109–119` | HIGH | pyext |
| 13 | `GcTraverse` calls `Py_VISIT` inside `classes_by_descriptor_mutex`; GC visitor callbacks may re-enter Python, trigger GC collection, or deadlock on the same mutex | `pyext/message_factory.cc:99–107` | HIGH | pyext |
| 14 | 9 `FreeThreadingTest`/`ThreadSafeTest` cases permanently `@skipIf(upb)`: dealloc race, lazy-init race, sub-message access, `GetOptions`, `GetFieldValue` | `python/.../thread_safe_test.py` | HIGH | UPB |
| 15 | `PyUpb_WeakMap_Next` calls `PyUnstable_TryIncRef` then immediately `Py_DECREF`s, returning a borrowed ref; the WeakMap mutex IS held for the entire iteration, so `WeakMap_Delete` is blocked — but if an external thread drops the last strong ref after `Py_DECREF` and `tp_dealloc` tears down object state before calling `WeakMap_Delete`, the iterating thread holds a borrowed ref to a partially-torn-down object; correct fix is to yield a `+1` reference | `python/protobuf.c:244–258` | HIGH | UPB |
| 16 | `PyUpb_WeakMap_Delete` silently no-ops on a stale iterator if two threads both trigger `Reify` on the same child; second `DeleteIter` leaves `upb_inttable` in inconsistent state | `python/protobuf.c:193–203` | MEDIUM | UPB |

**Anything else we should know about your project / environment**

title: Out-of-bounds Read via Negative Size Parameter in MessageLite::ParseFromArray

title: protoc v35.1 fails to build on FreeBSD 15.0 and LLVM/Clang 19.1.7 with libc++
author: NuLL3rr0r
body:
**What version of protobuf and what language are you using?**

Version: v35.1

Language: C++

FreeBSD

LLVM/Clang and libc++ (system's default)

**What did you do?**

Steps to reproduce the behavior:

I have a Makefile that builds protoc like this:

```Makefile
		echo "$(FMT_HEADER_PREFIX) Configuring protoc sources..."; \
		echo ; \
		echo " - $${SOURCE_DIR}"; \
		echo " - $${BUILD_DIR}"; \
		echo ; \
		"$(CMAKE)" \
			-G "Ninja Multi-Config" \
			-DCMAKE_MAKE_PROGRAM="$(NINJA)" \
			-DCMAKE_CONFIGURATION_TYPES="Release" \
			-DCMAKE_INSTALL_PREFIX="$${STAGE_DIR}" \
			-DCMAKE_INSTALL_LIBDIR=lib \
			-S "$${SOURCE_DIR}" \
			-B "$${BUILD_DIR}" \
			-DCMAKE_PREFIX_PATH="$${ABSEIL_STAGE_DIR};$${ZLIB_STAGE_DIR}" \
			-Dutf8_range_ENABLE_TESTS=OFF \
			-Dutf8_range_ENABLE_INSTALL=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_INSTALL=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_TESTS=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_CONFORMANCE=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_EXAMPLES=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_PROTOBUF_BINARIES=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_PROTOC_BINARIES=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_LIBPROTOC=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_LIBUPB=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_DISABLE_RTTI=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_TEST_XML_OUTDIR=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_ALLOW_CCACHE=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_FORCE_FETCH_DEPENDENCIES=OFF \
			-Dprotobuf_LOCAL_DEPENDENCIES_ONLY=ON \
			-Dprotobuf_BUILD_SHARED_LIBS=OFF \

			-Dprotobuf_WITH_ZLIB_DEFAULT=ON \
```

title: Tt
author: renatojd2002-commits
body:
> The new version of java lite has some incompatible changes with the previous version. We are considering how to roll it out. 

 _Originally posted by @TeBoring in [#5196](https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/issues/5196#issuecomment-426056581)_

title: [PHP] add a recursion_limit option to mergeFromString / serializeToString
author: norberttech
body:
**What language does this apply to?**
PHP 

**Describe the problem you are trying to solve.**

PHP is the only binding that won't let you raise the nesting depth limit and I keep hitting it. Ruby has it (#9218), Java/C++/C# have it too - PHP just hardcodes it. 

In the C ext mergeFromString/serializeToString pass the upb options as 0, and pure-PHP CodedInputStream fixes DEFAULT_RECURSION_LIMIT = 100 with no setter. So if a message goes deeper than ~100 levels you're stuck, nothing short of patching the runtime.

[PHP bindings for libpg_query](https://github.com/flow-php/pg-query-ext) push postgres parse trees through protobuf. 
A medium size/large query (25+ joins, or nested CASE/COALESCE) goes past 100 levels and dies on both decode and encode:

```
Error occurred during parsing   // mergeFromString
Max nesting exceeded            // serializeToString
```

Same thing the ruby pg_query gem hit and fixed with recursion_limit: 1000 after #9218 (pganalyze/pg_query#337). PHP has no way to do that.


**Describe the solution you'd like**

What I'd add (matching the other langs - `recursion_limit`):

```php
<?php 

$msg->mergeFromString($data, $recursion_limit = 100);
$msg->serializeToString($recursion_limit = 100);
```

upb already supports it (upb_DecodeOptions_MaxDepth + the encode equivalent), so it's just threading the option through. Pure-PHP already tracks the budget, just needs a setter for the starting value. Additive optional arg, default = current behavior, nothing breaks.

I'd keep it to the binary paths for now since that's what I need - can do mergeFromJsonString too if you want it.

**Describe alternatives you've considered**

I don't really see any alternatives 

**Additional context**

Before I write anything - would you take this PR? Happy to match whatever name/default/scope keeps it consistent with the other languages.

title: Java JSON serializer emits invalid extension keys by default
author: mkruskal-google
body:
The Java JSON serializer emits extensions using their short names here:
https://github.com/protocolbuffers/protobuf/blob/382f6a7eaf00f120afbc800723af7d67dd06ab63/java/util/src/main/java/com/google/protobuf/util/JsonFormat.java#L1175

For example, an extension `foo.bar` will be emitted as `"bar": "value"` instead of `"[foo.bar]": "value"`.

The resulting JSON is unparseable in any language, unless the extension's short name happens to clash with another field name.  In that case it will result in silent data corruption.  It can also lead to JSON with duplicate keys (i.e. data loss) if two extensions have the same short name.

As of 35.0 this has a fix that can be opted into by calling `printingFullyQualifiedExtensionNames()`.  But it can't be enabled by default without a breaking change

title: protobuf 6 silently rewrites pb2 files generated by protobuf 5, removing dependency imports
author: timvw
body:
## Description

When upgrading `protobuf` from 5.x to 6.x in an environment that has pb2 files generated by protoc 5.26.1, protobuf 6 silently rewrites the pb2 files and removes the explicit dependency import lines. This causes `AddSerializedFile()` to fail because the descriptor pool doesn't have the dependency protos registered.

## Example

A pb2 file generated by protoc 5.26.1 (`trace_service_pb2.py` from `opentelemetry-proto`) contains:

```python
from opentelemetry.proto.trace.v1 import trace_pb2 as opentelemetry_dot_proto_dot_trace_dot_v1_dot_trace__pb2

DESCRIPTOR = _descriptor_pool.Default().AddSerializedFile(b'...')
```

After installing protobuf 6.x, the file is rewritten to:

```python
DESCRIPTOR = _descriptor_pool.Default().AddSerializedFile(b'...')
```

The import line is gone. `AddSerializedFile()` then fails:

```
TypeError: Couldn't build proto file into descriptor pool:
Depends on file 'opentelemetry/proto/trace/v1/trace.proto', but it has not been loaded
```

## Reproduction

```bash
python -m venv /tmp/repro-venv
/tmp/repro-venv/bin/pip install 'protobuf>=5,<6' 'opentelemetry-proto==1.39.1'

# Before: pb2 file has dependency import (2698 bytes)
wc -c /tmp/repro-venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/opentelemetry/proto/collector/trace/v1/trace_service_pb2.py
grep 'import trace_pb2' /tmp/repro-venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/opentelemetry/proto/collector/trace/v1/trace_service_pb2.py

# Upgrade protobuf
/tmp/repro-venv/bin/pip install 'protobuf>=6,<7'

# After: dependency import stripped (2588 bytes)
wc -c /tmp/repro-venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/opentelemetry/proto/collector/trace/v1/trace_service_pb2.py
grep 'import trace_pb2' /tmp/repro-venv/lib/python3.*/site-packages/opentelemetry/proto/collector/trace/v1/trace_service_pb2.py

# Import fails
/tmp/repro-venv/bin/python -c "from opentelemetry.proto.collector.trace.v1 import trace_service_pb2"
```

## Expected behavior

Either:
1. protobuf 6 should not rewrite pb2 files generated by protobuf 5
2. If it does rewrite them, it should preserve the dependency imports (or use a mechanism that resolves dependencies automatically)

## Environment

- Python: 3.12
- protobuf 5: 5.29.6
- protobuf 6: 6.33.6
- opentelemetry-proto: 1.39.1
- OS: macOS 15.5 / Darwin 25.4.0
